coredata.dc:title: A TiO2 nanotube array decorated by Ag nanoparticles for highly sensitive SERS determination and self-cleaning of vitamin B12
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Mohammad
Mousavi, Mitra
Shirzad, Kamal
Hosseini, Monireh-Alsadat
Badiei, Alireza
Pourhakkak, Pouran
Ghasemi, Jahan B.
coredata.dc:description: The Ag-deposited TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNTA/Ag) are prepared via the decoration of Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on an anodized TiO2 nanotube. Descriptive physicochemical features of prepared probes were studied by XRD, FESEM, and RAMAN analyses to prove the successful construction of ordered TNTA and decoration of Ag NPs over them. A highly appropriate and efficient determination method based on surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has been developed using TNTA/Ag probe for vitamin B12 detection. The results displayed that the TNTA/Ag probe has superior detection sensitivity with detection limit of as low as 2.48 × 10−8 M and high reproducibility of different spots on the probe surface with a relative standard deviation of 9.69 %. Moreover, the prepared probe could clean itself due to the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 and surface plasmon resonance effect (SPR) of Ag NPs to be repeatedly used as a SERS probe. The reliability of TNTA/Ag SERS probe was affirmed further by the detection of vitamin B12 in real pharmaceutical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)
TiO2 nanotube arrays
Silver nanoparticles
Self-cleaning SERS
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thankfully acknowledge the University of Tehran for providing the research facility and funding to carry out this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of different samples: Metallic Ti, Anodized Ti, TNTA, and TNTA/Ag.
(a) FESEM image of bare Ti wire, (b, c) top-view and (d-g) cross-section FESEM images of TNTA (h, i) top-view FESEM images of TNTA/Ag, (j) EDX spectra and (k-n) EDX mapping images of TNTA/Ag.
(a) Comparison of the 10−5 M vitamin B12 Raman spectra on different SERS probes, (b) comparison of the Raman spectrum of vitamin B12 powder with SERS bands over TNTA/Ag.

(a) SERS spectra of vitamin B12 aqueous solution with different concentrations (a: 5 × 10−5, b: 10−5, c: 5 × 10−6, d: 10−6, e: 5 × 10−7, f: 10−7, g: 5 × 10−8) adsorbed on TNTA/Ag probe. (b) Linear trend of the dependence of SERS intensities at 1506 cm−1 on the logarithm concentration of vitamin B12.
(a) Time-dependent photocatalytic degradation of vitamin B12 (10−5 M) absorbed on the TNTA/Ag probe, and (b) recyclable SERS detection of vitamin B12 for 4 runs.
SERS detection of vitamin B12 over the TNTA/Ag probe.
Comparison of the analytical performance of this work with other various substrates/methods for the Determination of vitamin B12.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 1 Fluorescence quenching, 2 high-performance liquid chromatography, 3 hydrophilicionicliquid(IL)1-hexyl-3-methylimidazoliumchloride-K2HPO4, 4 aqueous two-phase system, 5 Differential pulse voltammetry, 6 X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy, 7 solid phase extraction, 8 high-performance liquid chromatography with diode array detection, 9 magnetic solid phase extraction, 10 differential pulse anodic voltammetry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of vitamin B12 in real pharmaceutical samples (injection and tablet)*.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * The confidence level was 95% for all reported values.
1Injection (EXIR pharmaceutical company), 2 injection (Darou Pakhsh pharmaceutical company), 3 injection (Pars Behrouzan Jam pharmaceutical company), 4 tablet (Euro OTC Pharma GmbH).

head.ce:title: A TiO2 nanotube array decorated by Ag nanoparticles for highly sensitive SERS determination and self-cleaning of vitamin B12

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), P.O. Box 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), P.O. Box 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Ag-deposited TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNTA/Ag) are prepared via the decoration of Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on an anodized TiO2 nanotube. Descriptive physicochemical features of prepared probes were studied by XRD, FESEM, and RAMAN analyses to prove the successful construction of ordered TNTA and decoration of Ag NPs over them. A highly appropriate and efficient determination method based on surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has been developed using TNTA/Ag probe for vitamin B12 detection. The results displayed that the TNTA/Ag probe has superior detection sensitivity with detection limit of as low as 2.48 × 10−8 M and high reproducibility of different spots on the probe surface with a relative standard deviation of 9.69 %. Moreover, the prepared probe could clean itself due to the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 and surface plasmon resonance effect (SPR) of Ag NPs to be repeatedly used as a SERS probe. The reliability of TNTA/Ag SERS probe was affirmed further by the detection of vitamin B12 in real pharmaceutical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B12 as a member of the group B vitamins is a water-soluble cobalt-containing vitamin known as cobalamin [1–3]. The major forms of cobalamins are cyanocobalamin, methylcobalamin, adenosylcobalamin, and hydroxycobalamin. Each form has its special biological activity and stability [4,5]. Amongst them, cyanocobalamin is the most stable active cobalamin. Vitamin B12 is a substantial vitamin for the human diet, which is not made by the body and is essential for the formation of red blood cells, the synthesis of DNA, and the preservation of neurological operations. This vitamin is found in foods of animal origin like meat, fish, oysters, liver, eggs, milk, and clams, and its deficiency leads to health problems such as anemia, weight loss, nausea, and fatigue. Also, vitamin B12 is added to some pharmaceutical products like multivitamin tablets, capsules and injections, energy drinks, and toothpaste to provide for vegetarians' needs [6–8]. So, it is important to develop simple and sensitive strategies for the detection of vitamin B12 in different targets. Diverse analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography [9], colorimetric [10], mass spectrometry [11], chemiluminescence, fluorimetric [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], amperometric [14], and atomic absorption spectrometry [15] have been applied for the determination of vitamin B12. These techniques have a significant disadvantage in the total analysis time to gain the results. So, the development of a specific and fast method would be useful. One method is using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [16].

In recent years, Raman spectroscopy has been utilized as a nondestructive analysis tool to get molecule-specific data about the chemical compound [17,18]. However, detecting the low concentration of molecules is difficult due to the weak Raman signal from molecules. Different enhancement approaches like surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, tip-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, and resonance Raman spectroscopy have been applied to increase the sensitivity of Raman spectroscopy [19–21]. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has received much attention as a promising technique in various analytical fields such as medicine, chemistry, biology, and catalysis because of its nondestructive, high sensitivity, good convenience, molecular fingerprint, and quick response [16,22–24]. SERS techniques for the enhancement of the Raman intensity are based on two mechanisms: (I) Electromagnetic mechanism (EM) that is attributed to the localized surface plasmons on noble metals such as Au and Ag under light excitation, and (II) Chemical mechanism (CM) that is associated to electron transfer between SERS probe and substrate molecule, molecule resonance, and interfacial non-resonant interactions. So, both the probe and substrate are important in CM [25,26].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the most attractive metal oxides because of its significant photocatalytic ability, high chemical stability, suitable structural rigidity, relatively low cost, and good biocompatibility [27]. It has been utilized in different applications like photoelectrochemistry, photocatalysis, dye-sensitized solar cells, Li-ion batteries, photovoltaic, antireflection coatings, gas detection, self-cleaning, and chemiresistors [28–31]. TiO2 can be constructed in diverse morphologies containing nanoparticles, nanorods, nanotubes, nanowires, nanocubes, nanosponges, and nanosheets, with various surface-to-volume ratios an important factor for their applications [32,33]. Between different morphologies, TiO2 nanotubes arrays (TNTA) have received remarkable attention due to the large surface area, unique physical and chemical properties, and one-dimensional nanostructure with tubular symmetries. TNTA may be synthesized using the hydrothermal method, pattern technique, sol–gel process, electrospinning, and electrochemical anodization [34,35]. One of the simple, cheapest, and most commonly utilized techniques is electrochemical anodizing in fluoride-based electrolytes upon the applied voltage [36]. The morphological features such as wall thickness, diameter, height, and uniformity of TNTA can be carefully controlled by optimizing anodizing conditions like anodization time, electrolyte composition, temperature, and anodic voltage. In addition, the conductivity of TNTA can be improved by the conversion of the mainly amorphous structure into the crystalline form of rutile or anatase via thermal processing [37,38]. However, TNTA suffers from disadvantages like limited light absorption range due to wide bandgap and easy recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs at the surface, limiting their applications in many aspects [39]. These shortcomings have been addressed in different modification strategies such as sensitization by metals or non-metals doping, noble metal deposition, and other suitable semiconductors coupling [40]. Ag nanoparticle (Ag NPs) has a low cost compared to the other noble metals and show a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) for the extension of the light absorption to longer wavelengths (visible region) and act as sinks for the rapid electron separation [41,42].

Within this contribution, we present a reusable SERS-active substrate composed of Ag NPs deposited TNTA (TNTA/Ag) to determine vitamin B12. The TNTA/Ag arrays showed stronger Raman intensity than TNTA. For practical detections, the TNTA/Ag probe exhibited superior detection sensitivity for vitamin B12 with LOD of 2.48 × 10−8 M. After detecting vitamin B12 based on the SERS application, the probe can clean itself by photocatalytic degradation of vitamin B12 absorbing on the surface because of the catalytic abilities of the TNTA/Ag. In addition, the composite arrays as reproducible, stable, and highly sensitive SERS-active probes are discussed in detail via different experiments.

Bare Ti wire with a high degree of purity and a thickness of 2 mm was purchased from Nazari Company (Iran). Glucose (C6H12O6, >99 %), Ammonia (NH3, 25 %), Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, >99 %), Ammonium fluoride (NH4F, >99 %), Nitric acid (HNO3, 65 %), Hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40 %), were acquired from Merck. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, >99 %), and Vitamin B12 (C63H88CoN14O14P, >98 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

TNTA was prepared by the anodization method. Firstly, titanium wires were polished mechanically with sandpaper (1000) to reduce the surface roughness. The chemical polishing was realized by placing the wires in a mixture of hydrofluoric acid and nitric acid for 15 s. The polished wires were ultrasonicated in acetone and deionized water for 5 min to remove surface oil and oxide and then washed with pure water and dried with argon gas flow. The anodization procedure was done with a two-electrode electrochemical cell. A graphite rod was utilized as a cathode, a titanium wire as an anode, and an ethylene glycol solution of ammonium fluoride as an electrolytic solution. The reaction was carried out with a potential of 60 V for 30 min after commencing power. Afterward, the titanium wires were washed with acetone and deionized water and calcinated at 450 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. Finally, the TNTA was produced on the titanium wire surface. In the next step, for the fabrication of Ag-doped TNTA, a silver nitrate solution containing 0.2 M Ag(NO3), 0.4 M NH3, and 0.3 M glucose was prepared. The TNTA was immersed in the prepared silver solution for 3 times, each lasting 1 min. Finally, the electrodes were rinsed with deionized water and dried with argon gas flow (Scheme 1 ).

X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV) were obtained to investigate the phase structure of samples. The morphology and structure of materials were identified by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi-S4160) equipped with a dispersive energy X-ray (EDX) analyzer to determine the elemental composition. UV–vis absorption spectra of the vitamin B12 samples were recorded by a Photonix Ar 2015 spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were collected using a Teksan Raman spectrometer (Takram) with the excitation wavelength of 532 nm DPSS Nd:YAG (cw) laser line at room temperature. The laser power was about 100 mW.

Firstly, the powder of vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) was used to prepare a solution with a concentration of 10−3 M. Then, different solutions with concentrations of 5 × 10−8, 10−7, 5 × 10−7, 10−6, 5 × 10−6, 10−5, and 5 × 10−5 were prepared b successive dilution of the above solution and stored in the refrigerator.

A 0.5 µL of prepared vitamin B12 solution was dropped on the SERS probe and then placed under a Raman microscope with an excitation laser of 532 nm. To decrease the photocatalytic degradation caused by laser illumination, the laser beam was focused to a size of 2 μm with low power of 10 mW. The collection time of a single Raman spectrum was 1 s. The Raman spectra of samples were acquired by measuring diverse positions on the probes (Scheme 2 ).

The crystallinity and phase composition of the different samples were assessed by X-ray diffractometry. According to Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks of pure metallic Ti and anodized Ti at 35.08°, 38.38°, 40.12°, and 53.09° were ascribed to the (100), (002), (101), and (102) crystal planes of Ti, respectively (JCPDS no. 44–1294) [43]. As observed, the patterns of annealed anodized-Ti (TNTA) match well with metallic phase Ti and anatase-phase TiO2 (25.22°, 48.12°, 54.10°, and 55.11° corresponded to the (101), (200), (105), and (211) planes, respectively with JCPDS no. 21–1272), which depicted the formation of anatase phase [44]. Compared with TNTA, two additional peaks at 38.06° and 44.28° belong to (111) and (200) crystal planes of Ag could be seen in the TNTA/Ag sample (JCPDS no. 65–2871) [45]. In addition, the peaks attributed to the (220) and (311) planes of Ag cannot be observed due to the dispersion and small size of Ag NPs on the surface of TNTA.

The morphological features of the bare Ti wire, TNTA, and TNTA/Ag samples were evaluated by FESEM analysis. Fig. 2 (a) shows that the surface morphology of the pure Ti wire is even, and there is no porous structure. Fig. 2 (b, c) present the top view FESEM images of the TNTA sample. As seen in figure, the dense TNTA formed, and there was no lump aggregated over the pores. The cross-sectional views of the sample were exhibited in Fig. 2 (d-g). The presence of regular and highly ordered nanotubular structures can be noticed. It can be seen that the nanotube walls were separated from each other, and they are open at the top and closed at the bottom. Fig. 2 (h, i) show the FESEM images of TNTA decorated with Ag NPs. From these figures, most Ag NPs deposited on the top of nanotubes with a small amount loaded into the nanotube structure.

EDX analysis was conducted to investigate the purity and elemental composition of TNTA/Ag hybrid, as displayed in Fig. 2 (j). It is possible to see that the elements of the TNTA/Ag included Ti, O, and Ag. Moreover, the EDX mapping was applied to ensure the distribution of elements in TNTA/Ag. The uniform distribution of different colors in the images (Fig. 2 (k-n)) proved that Ti, O, and Ag distribution is uniform.

Raman spectroscopy was also conducted to appraise the chemical composition information. As observed in Fig. S1, there was no distinct Raman peak for the anodized-Ti sample, which indicates that it is amorphous. At the same time, characteristic Raman bands of the anatase phase have appeared in the TNTA spectrum at 145, 199, 398, 518, and 639 cm−1 approving the transformation of amorphous Ti into crystalline anatase phase after the annealing process. These five Raman peaks can be assigned to the active modes of anatase: 3Eg + 2B1g + A1g modes. The main peak at 145 cm−1 and two other peaks at 199 and 639 cm − 1 attributed to the Eg mode originated from the symmetric stretching vibration of O-Ti-O. Another peak at 398 cm−1 ascribed to the B1g mode corresponds to the symmetric bending vibration of O-Ti-O, while the peak at 518 cm−1 assigned to the A1g + B1g mode is due to the antisymmetric vibration of O-Ti-O [46,47]. The obtained results agree with the XRD results, which confirmed TiO2 anatase as the major crystalline component.

The SERS performances of as-constructed probes are studied with vitamin B12 as the target molecule via a series of designed experiments to appraise their potential as a SERS probe. Fig. 3 a compares the Raman spectra of 10−5 M vitamin B12 over Anodized-Ti, TNTA, and TNTA/Ag. We also prepared the anodized aluminum foil as the SERS probe for comparison. According to obtained spectra, no apparent Raman signal was observed over anodized aluminum foil, Anodized-Ti, and TNTA probes. At the same time, the TNTA/Ag hybrid displayed a significant Raman intensity, suggesting this material has ideal Raman susceptibility that is assigned to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effects by deposited Ag NPs and charge transfer (CT) by TiO2 semiconductor. These outcomes evidence that Ag NPs over the TNTA have a pivotal impact on enhancing the Raman signal of vitamin B12. All the observed SERS bands over TNTA/Ag can be ascribed entirely to the characteristic peaks of vitamin B12 compared to the standard Raman spectrum of vitamin B12 powder (Fig. 3b) [48].

The sensitivity of TNTA/Ag as an optimal SERS probe was investigated by detecting different concentrations of vitamin B12 from 5 × 10−5 to 5 × 10−8 M, with a very low detection limit. From Fig. 4 a, the SERS spectra of vitamin B12 at various concentrations enhanced by TNTA/Ag show a distinct peak at 1506 cm−1 [49]. The Raman intensity is reduced with the decrease of substrate concentration, but there is a peak that can be seen at a concentration as low as 10−8 M. As observed in Fig. 4b, the SERS intensity of the characteristic band at 1506 cm−1 showed a supreme linear relationship with the logarithm of concentrations of vitamin B12. The linear equation is Y = 300.73X + 2190.2, where X is the logarithm of vitamin B12 concentration, Y is Raman band intensity at 1506 cm−1, and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9747. According to the IUPAC recommendation (the minimally signal strength must be higher than threefold the background signal deviation) [50], the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the calibration curve equation as follows. The values of the intercept and standard deviation of the intercept of the calibration line were taken as blank signal and standard deviation of the blank signal, respectively. In the current case, the x-intercept and its standard deviation were used to calculate the detection limit. Notably, the concentration corresponding to the detection limit was rescaled as the calibration line was in the log scale of the concentration. The obtained value is 2.48 × 10−8 M, demonstrating that this SERS probe is highly sensitive and effective for trace and quantitative analysis.

Moreover, different concentration of vitamin B12 adsorbed on TNTA/Ag was monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy to verify further the accuracy of SERS detection method. The obtained results in Fig. S2 (a) show that the intensity of the main UV–vis absorption peak of vitamin B12 at 361 nm [6] was decreased as the concentration reduced in the range 5 × 10−5 M to 10−6 M and almost disappeared at concentrations below 10−6. The relationship between absorbance and concentrations is exhibited in Fig. S2 (b). The linear dynamic range and detection limit of UV–Vis spectroscopy is smaller, about 100 times the corresponding values obtained by SERS method.

In addition to sensitivity, the reproducibility of SERS detection is another crucial factor in evaluating a SERS probe. To assess the uniformity and signal reproducibility of the constructed TNTA/Ag probe, we selected 9 random spots and recorded the vitamin B12 SERS spectrum at these points. Fig. S3 (a) displays the Raman peak intensity of vitamin B12 at 1506 cm−1 for various data points. The relative standard deviation (RSD at 95 % of confidence level) value was estimated as 9.69 %, indicating the superior uniformity of the TNTA/Ag probe (Fig. S3 (b)) [41,50]. The outstanding reproducibility of prepared TNTA/Ag can be assigned to the successful preparation of ordered TNTA and uniform deposition of Ag NPs over the TNTA. The results confirm that the prepared TNTA/Ag probe can be used for SERS detection.

The analytical performance of the proposed substrate and detection method (TNTA/Ag substrate and SERS method) for determination of vitamin B12 was compared with other substrates in other developed methods and the results are summarized in Table 1
[3,51–56]. Obviousely, the sensitivity of the proposed TNTA/Ag substrate for the vitamin B12 analyte by the present SERS method is comparable with those obtained by other methods. Moreover, the results reveal that this proposed SERS probe has not only excellent dynamic linear range, but also show significant recovery percent that make it suitable for practical applications. All of the results prove the advantages and significant improvement in the current work in analytical point of view.

As known, TiO2 is a semiconductor with the photocatalytic ability and can be used as a self-cleaning of vitamin B12 contaminations [57]. So, in addition to SERS performance, the photocatalytic activity of the TNTA/Ag probe was monitored for degradation of vitamin B12 by Raman spectra. For this purpose, an as-prepared TNTA/Ag probe was applied to degrade vitamin B12 by using Xe-lamp irradiation. Target molecules were first adsorbed on the probe, then irradiated by Xe light at different times. Finally, the photocatalytic degradation process was monitored by SERS spectra using the Raman spectrophotometer. The results in Fig. 5 a exhibited that the intensity of the characteristic peak at 1506 cm−1 dramatically decreased and finally disappeared after 75 min Xe-lamp irradiation, suggesting vitamin B12 target molecules on the TNTA/Ag probe were degraded entirely by the photocatalytic reaction. The excellent photocatalytic activity of TNTA/Ag can be related to the generation of electrons on the TiO2 surface, the transfer of the generated electrons between TiO2 and Ag NPs through the interface, and the surface plasmon resonance effect of Ag particles [41]. Therefore, based on the remarkable photocatalytic ability of TNTA/Ag, it is expected to have good recyclability via self-cleaning function. The recyclability of the prepared probe was assessed by repeating the adsorption and photodegradation processes [58]. In this way, after detecting vitamin B12, the surface of the probe was cleaned by photocatalytic decomposing of target molecules. As observed in Fig. 5b, an as-constructed TNTA/Ag probe can be employed to detect vitamin B12 for 4 cycles without notably losing SERS ability. These outcomes confirmed the excellent photocatalytic ability of TNTA and the stability of Ag nanoparticles and showed that the TNTA/Ag could be utilized frequently as SERS substrate.

In order to illustrate the practical applications of TNTA/Ag probe, we applied it in the detection of vitamin B12 in real samples. First, the pharmaceutical injection and tablet samples were selected as the target to conduct vitamin B12 detection. The SERS probe was drop-coated with specific and different amounts of model samples solution and then was dried for the later SERS evaluations. Each experiment was repeated three time (n = 3). The obtained SERS data of selected targets were tabulated in Table 2 . As observed, low vitamin B12 concentration of 10−8 M can be easily detected by SERS method over constructed probe with acceptable recovery and RSD values. So, the feasibility and reliability of the developed TNTA/Ag SERS probe were confirmed by the sensitive and accurate detection of B12 in real pharmaceutical samples. In addition, the ICP-OES method was applied to the real samples to approve the accuracy of the proposed method, and the obtained results are listed in Table 2. As it can be seen, the results obtained by the SERS method are close to a well-established method, ICP-OES. The differences between the results of the proposed SERS and ICP-OES methods are in the acceptable limits encountered in experimental measurements.

In conclusion, titanium nanotube arrays were obtained via anodizing-calcinating of titanium wire. Then Ag NPs uniformly deposited on the ordered 3D nanotubes to fabricate TNTA/Ag. The prepared sample was used as a SERS probe for quantitative and sensitive detection and measurement of vitamin B12. The experiments reveal that the TNTA/Ag probe has higher SERS activity than Anodized-Ti and TNTA probes which could be attributed to the SPR effect of Ag NPs, CT effect of TiO2, and synergistic effect of formed junction between TiO2 and Ag NPs. In addition, the SERS intensity over TNTA/Ag and vitamin B12 concentration show a significant linear relationship in the range 5 × 10−5 to 5 × 10−8 M. The quantitative determination of vitamin B12 showed an acceptable LOD of 2.48 × 10−8 M. The reproducibility of signals and uniformity of probe's surface were successfully realized with RSD less than 10 %. Notably, the TNTA/Ag probe illustrates excellent self-cleaning activity and can be reused for 4 runs without remarkably losing its SERS performance. This research will help design SERS probes based on TiO2 semiconductor and SPR generating materials for effectively sensing B12.
Mohammad Jafari, Mitra Mousavi, Kamal Shirzad, and Monire Sadat Hosseini processed the experimental data, performed the analysis, drafted the manuscript and designed the figures.
Mitra Mousavi, Alireza Badiei, Jahan B. Ghasemi and Pouran Pourhakkak were involved in planning and supervised the work, worked on the manuscript.
All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thankfully acknowledge the University of Tehran for providing the research facility and funding to carry out this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107813.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A one-pot synthesis of fluorescent N, P-codoped carbon dots for vitamin B12 determination and bioimaging application
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, X. Mei, S. Wang, J. Li, C. Dong, A one-pot synthesis of fluorescent N, P-codoped carbon dots for vitamin B12 determination and bioimaging application, New Journal of Chemistry 45 (2021) 3508-3514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots doped with phosphorus and nitrogen are a viable fluorescent nanoprobe for determination and cellular imaging of vitamin B12 and cobalt (II)
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, H. Wang, Q. Hu, X. Guo, L. Li, S. Shuang, X. Gong, C. Dong, Carbon quantum dots doped with phosphorus and nitrogen are a viable fluorescent nanoprobe for determination and cellular imaging of vitamin B12 and cobalt (II), Microchimica Acta 186 (2019) 1-13.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trace determination of vitamin B12 in food samples by using Fe3O4 magnetic particles including multi-walled carbon nanotubes and nanodiamonds
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.I. Ulusoy, S. Gülle, E. Yilmaz, M. Soylak, Trace determination of vitamin B12 in food samples by using Fe3O4 magnetic particles including multi-walled carbon nanotubes and nanodiamonds, Analytical Methods 11 (2019) 5108-5117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical vitamin sensors: A critical review
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Huang, S. Tian, W. Zhao, K. Liu, J. Guo, Electrochemical vitamin sensors: A critical review, Talanta 222 (2021) 121645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile and economic electrochemical sensor for methylmalonic acid: A potential biomarker for vitamin B12 deficiency
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Rajeev, L. Benny, M. Roy, A.T. Mathew, K.B. Akshaya, A. Varghese, G. Hegde, A facile and economic electrochemical sensor for methylmalonic acid: A potential biomarker for vitamin B12 deficiency, New Journal of Chemistry 46 (2022) 4114-4125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electrochemical strategy for determination of vitamin B12 by Co (I/II) redox pair monitoring with boron-doped diamond electrode
Diam. Relat. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of vitamin B12 in human plasma and urine samples using silver nanoparticles embedded in chitosan in micellar media
Anal. Methods

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots nanosensor for label-free determination of vitamin B12 based on inner filter effect
Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ding, H. Yang, S. Ge, J. Yu, Fluorescent carbon dots nanosensor for label-free determination of vitamin B12 based on inner filter effect, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy 193 (2018) 305-309.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of vitamin B12 in four edible insect species by immunoaffinity and ultra-high performance liquid chromatography
